title: 25 Nehase — St. Tekle Haymanot & The Assembly
narrative:
Nehase

St. Tekle Haymanot of Ethiopia, Departure

St. Titus the Apostle

St. Bartholomew the Apostle (Monthly Commemoration)

On the twenty-fifth day of Nehase, the Ethiopian Church stands at one of the most sacred and beloved moments in her entire calendar: the Feast of the Departure of St. Tekle Haymanot, the Light of Ethiopia, the apostle of the South, the monk of incomparable austerity and boundless pastoral love whose life and witness have shaped the Ethiopian Church perhaps more profoundly than any other single figure since the Nine Saints brought the Gospel to the northern highlands. Tekle Haymanot was born around 1215 A.D. in the Shewa region, the son of a priest, and from his earliest years manifested a quality of spiritual intensity that set him apart from ordinary religious life. He received his formation from the greatest spiritual masters of his era, was ordained priest, and began a missionary work of extraordinary extent — traveling the length and breadth of Ethiopia, preaching, healing, casting out evil spirits, and drawing the people back to the faith of their fathers.

The sanctity of Tekle Haymanot is inseparable from his asceticism, which the tradition describes in terms that strain the limits of ordinary credulity and which the Church accepts as signs of the Spirit's extraordinary action in an extraordinary vessel. He is said to have stood in prayer on one leg for so long that the other leg withered and fell away — and the tradition has him stand thereafter on the remaining one, the body's limitation becoming the soul's emblem of single-minded orientation toward God. He fasted to extremities that would have destroyed an ordinary constitution, and yet he was not diminished but enlarged by these austerities — not made harsh but made tender, not turned inward but thrust outward in compassion for every soul he encountered. He founded the great monastery of Debre Libanos, which became and remains the spiritual heartland of Ethiopian Christianity, the mother of the monastic tradition in the center of the country, and the school in which countless generations of priests, deacons, and monks have been formed.

His intercessions were not confined to the living: tradition records that he descended into the realm of the dead — following in the footsteps of his Lord — and that by his prayers souls were released from the places of suffering into the light of Paradise. This extraordinary claim is not made carelessly by the Church; it is an expression of the Tewahedo conviction that the communion of saints is not a metaphor but a living reality, that the saints who have passed into the fullness of Christ's life continue to act in this world with a power that is greater, not lesser, than what they possessed in the body. When the faithful of Ethiopia call upon Tekle Haymanot, they call upon one who is alive, active, and near — the Light of Ethiopia burning as brightly in heaven as it ever did on the roads of Shewa.

St. Titus the Apostle and St. Bartholomew the Apostle join Tekle Haymanot in today's exalted assembly, weaving the particular glory of Ethiopia's greatest monastic into the global apostolic witness of the universal Church. Titus was the beloved companion of St. Paul, the faithful co-worker to whom one of the Pastoral Epistles is addressed, the first bishop of Crete, who spent his life translating the apostolic vision into the practical structures of communities across the Greek and Cretan world. His presence alongside Tekle Haymanot honors the continuity of the apostolic mission: what Titus did for Crete in the first century, Tekle Haymanot did for Ethiopia in the thirteenth, and the Spirit that animated both of them is one Spirit. St. Bartholomew, commemorated monthly, was the apostle whose mission tradition assigns in part to India and to Armenia — the great apostle of the edges of the known world — and whose monthly presence on the Ethiopian calendar acknowledges that the Church is always on the move toward those who have not yet heard. Together, on this twenty-fifth and luminous day of Nehase, these three apostles — one Ethiopian, one Pauline, one of the original Twelve — stand as witnesses to the single, universal, inexhaustible mission of Christ in the world.
